text: Hello, I'm sharing a recent case, kind of frustrating. This is a patient who six months ago I performed a

complicated low anterior resection for a case where the patient had had a sacral copeplexy about twenty years earlier that had eroded into the vagina and the bowel and I did a repair where I removed as much of the mesh as I could and did a sigmoid resection, vaginal closure, and a mental flap in the rectal vaginal septum.
And the patient did very well for about five, six months and then developed a recurrent colon to vaginal fistula.

On imaging, I couldn't really tell exactly where the fistula was coming from, although I knew that it was higher up, likely my anastomosis. There were no indications of an early post-op leak or any concerns prior to maybe five months later when she started developing drainage from the vagina.
And so I elected to take her back and try to remedy the situation.
And depending on the findings, either an anastomosis, likely with a diversion because there was a... pelvic abscess versus a Hartman procedure at the time of this operation.

The surgeon carefully dissects to locate the omental flap, which was previously sutured to the rectovaginal septum between the anastomosis and vaginal closure. The surgical field shows the area where a small bowel resection was performed during the first operation due to a fistula involving the small bowel and mesh.
The surgeon carefully dissects the appendix and small bowel, which are drawn into the area of the fistula on the right side, attempting to safely separate these structures.
The surgeon carefully detaches the omental flap from the surface of the colon, revealing the underlying tissue.
I was pleased to see that this actually stayed in location and served its purpose and you'll see later on that the fistula is not anterior, it's actually posterior.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the colon while searching for the ureter, ensuring its preservation during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the colon to right vaginal fistula and maneuvers to reposition the small bowel and appendix, clearing the view for better visualization of the fistula.
These are very challenging cases. These are the types of situations where many surgeons would choose to do open.
I can't fault them for that given the complexity. Minimally invasive techniques are certainly doable given your experience level.
The surgeon identifies the fistula connecting the colon to the vagina, revealing the cavity leading into the right side of the vagina. The small bowel is visibly adhered to the cavity.

I do feel that the robotic platform has advantages over laparoscopic just due to the control of the instrumentation as well as the tensile strength of the instruments and trying to do blunt dissection.
The surgeon mobilizes the small bowel from the retroperitoneum, revealing a prior small bowel anastomosis, which is examined and appears intact.

The surgeon carefully dissects the appendix, which is adhered to the right ureter and right common iliac artery. The use of stents with ICG aids in visualizing these structures. After successfully freeing the appendix, the surgeon performs an appendectomy due to its compromised condition.

When I do incidental appendectomies at surgeries like this, I try to decrease cost as much as I can, so I'll avoid using a stapler, and I'll simply ligate the base of the appendix with a silk tie. I'm only showing you one of my ties. I actually used two. I don't know if it matters. It makes me feel better.

The surgeon transects the appendix and begins to mobilize the colon, using a medial to lateral approach to lift the mesentery from the retroperitoneum, aware of the presence of an abscess in the area.
In the back of my mind, I was wondering if some residual mesh and or tax was the culprit for this happening. I clearly could not tell on imaging. So I'm here to find out for myself.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the iliac vessels to avoid presacral bleeding, focusing on the mesentery of the bowel. The right-sided abscess is visible, wrapping from posterior to the right side and entering the vagina. The origin of the bowel fistula is identified as posterior, prompting a shift from medial to lateral dissection.

The surgeon navigates through less inflamed tissue planes, dealing with post-operative adhesions. The use of ICG aids in speeding up the dissection process, allowing for quick identification of safe zones during the procedure.

The surgeon attempts to lift the lateral aspect of the old anastomosis from the sacrum. The anastomosis is sewn, connecting the two stapled ends of the bowel, with a collotomy and proctotomy made anteriorly, ensuring no staples are visible in the anterior section.

The surgeon carefully navigates below the anastomosis into the deeper pelvic region, lifting tissue off the sacrum. The posterior cavity is visible, indicating proximity to the fistula's origin. The bowel opening is observed, and robotic instruments are used to manage the tensile strength required for dissection. The deeper pelvic cavity extends to the right towards the vagina.
The surgeon carefully lifts the lateral aspect of the bowel anastomosis off the sacrum, using gentle back-and-forth movements to avoid creating a hole in the tissue.

The surgeon proceeds anteriorly, carefully dissecting deeper into the rectovaginal septum to ensure complete resolution of the colovaginal fistula. A clean tissue plane is visible during the dissection.

The surgeon performs proctoscopy and vaginoscopy to verify the position during dissection. The camera is positioned at thirty degrees up to provide a clear view of the deepest part of the rectovaginal septum dissection.

The surgeon carefully separates the vagina from the distal rectum, ensuring no structures are missed. The bladder and vagina are elevated. A leak test is performed, revealing bubbles emerging from the posterior aspect of the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a mesenteric resection, lifting tissue off the sacrum. An abscess cavity is visible, indicating the origin of the fistula. Mesh remnants are observed eroding into the bowel, contributing to the fistula formation.

The surgeon staples the bowel just distal to the prior anastomosis, confirmed via proctoscopy. The camera provides a high view as the surgeon carefully extracts mesh and numerous titanium tacks from the surgical site, avoiding bleeding. The posterior cavity is visible, wrapping around the right side and extending into the vagina.

The surgeon meticulously removes residual fibers of mesh from the pelvic cavity, ensuring no healthy bowel is placed over the area. The bone appears intact, reducing the risk of osteomyelitis. The surgical field is covered with omentum, and a colostomy is performed to prevent bowel movement through the affected area.

The surgeon marks the short rectal stump with a proline stitch for easier identification in future procedures. The surgeon inspects the vagina for potential repair, noting a pinhole defect that does not allow air passage, and decides to leave it untouched to avoid further injury. A pelvic drain and left upper quadrant colostomy are left in place.
